dashboard.nothingOmitted: Nothing omitted — all participant speech is in the report.

export.heading: Export report
export.subtitle: Creates a standalone report that anyone can open in a browser. Recipients can view and search but cannot edit.
export.anonymiseHint: Strips names from metadata, filenames, and speaker labels. Keeps participant codes (p1, p2). Moderator names are preserved. Names mentioned within quote text are NOT removed.
export.pasteHint: Paste into Miro, Excel, or Google Sheets

clips.piiWarning: Clips contain original audio — PII redaction applies to transcript text only
clips.ffmpegMissing: FFmpeg not found — install it to extract clips
clips.noClips: No starred or featured quotes to extract

export.savePanelMessage: Choose where to save the report.
export.saveFailedAlert: The export could not be saved.

miro.title: Send to Miro board
miro.connectIntro: Push your quotes onto a new Miro board as sticky notes, grouped by section and theme. Connect once — we never touch your existing boards.
miro.connectError: Could not connect — check the token and try again.
miro.oauthTimeout: Didn't hear back from Miro. Try again, or paste a token.
miro.oauthUnconfigured: OAuth isn't configured here. Paste an access token instead.
miro.exportError: Export failed. Check your connection and try again.
miro.linkClipsHint: Point at the folder where you placed the exported clips.
miro.uploadNotice: Exports all visible quotes. Data will be uploaded to Miro.

feedback.heading: How is Bristlenose working for you?
feedback.placeholder: Tell us what's useful and what needs fixing...
feedback.anonymous: Anonymous - only your rating and message are shared.
feedback.copiedToClipboard: Copied to clipboard - paste into an email or issue.
feedback.copyFailed: Could not copy — please submit feedback manually.

guide.intro: This guide explains how to read and work with your Bristlenose analysis.
guide.sectionsBody: Each quote is classified as screen-specific (about a particular screen or task) or general context (about the participant’s broader situation). Screen-specific quotes are grouped into sections; general quotes are grouped into emergent themes. Every quote appears in exactly one place.
guide.sentimentBody: Seven sentiment tags designed for UX research: frustration, confusion, doubt, surprise, satisfaction, delight, and confidence. Each quote receives one tag based on the participant’s expressed emotion.
guide.signalsBody: The analysis page surfaces signals — statistically notable concentrations of sentiment within report sections. Strong and moderate signals highlight where participant experience clusters.
guide.starsBody: Star quotes to mark important findings. Add tags to build your own coding scheme. Use the tag filter and search to focus on what matters. All changes are saved automatically.
guide.exportBody: Download a self-contained HTML report that works offline. The export preserves all your stars, tags, and edits.

contributing.licence: Bristlenose is free and open source under AGPL-3.0 with a Contributor Licence Agreement (CLA).
contributing.beforeBody: Run ruff check ., pytest tests/, and npm run build (tsc catches type errors Vitest misses). Version lives in bristlenose/__init__.py — bump with scripts/bump-version.py.
contributing.translateBody: Help translate Bristlenose into your language on Weblate. No Git or JSON knowledge required.

acknowledgements.intro: Bristlenose is built with these open-source projects.

codebook.sectionsBody: Each quote is classified as either screen-specific (about a particular screen or task) or general context (about the participant’s broader situation). Screen-specific quotes are grouped by screen; general-context quotes are grouped by emergent theme. Every quote appears in exactly one section of the report — no duplicates.
codebook.themesBody: Themes are inductive: they emerge from the data rather than being imposed from a fixed taxonomy, following Braun & Clarke’s (2006) thematic analysis. A theme requires at least two quotes — a single quote is an observation, not a pattern.
codebook.quoteEthicsBody: Quotes follow a principle of “dignity without distortion” — filler words are removed, light grammar fixes applied, but meaning and emotional register are never changed. Clarifying context is added in [square brackets] only when needed. Researcher questions that precede a quote are preserved as context prefixes.
codebook.sentimentBody: Seven sentiment categories are applied during quote extraction: frustration, confusion, doubt, surprise, satisfaction, delight, and confidence. Each has an intensity scale of 1–3. Quotes with no emotional content receive no sentiment tag. See the Signals section for the full taxonomy and how sentiment concentration is computed.
codebook.frameworkBody: Four codebooks are available for autocode tagging. Each defines a set of code groups with tag definitions that an LLM uses to classify quotes. Tags are applied with a confidence score; the researcher reviews and accepts or rejects proposals.
codebook.garrettDesc: Five-layer model from abstract strategy to concrete surface design: Strategy, Scope, Structure, Skeleton, Surface. Each layer depends on the one below — a top-down hierarchy of UX decisions. 5 code groups, 20 tags.
codebook.normanDesc: Seven interaction design principles: Discoverability, Feedback, Conceptual Model, Signifiers, Mapping, Constraints, Slips vs Mistakes. A bottom-up diagnostic framework that names the moment of interaction failure. 7 code groups, 26 tags.
codebook.platonicDesc: Six domains of Platonic philosophy: Theory of Forms, Knowledge & Opinion, Dialectic Method, Soul & Psychology, Ethics & Virtue, Argumentation & Irony. For analysing philosophical texts rather than product interviews. 6 code groups, 30 tags.
codebook.bristlenoseDesc: Eight universal qualitative research patterns: Behaviour, Pain points, Needs & desires, Mental models, Motivation, Emotion & trust, Context, Learning. Domain-agnostic practitioner vocabulary that works across any research study. 8 code groups, 45 tags.
codebook.citBraun2006: Flexible six-phase method for identifying patterns in qualitative data — inductive (data-driven) or deductive (theory-driven).
codebook.citSaldana2021: The standard reference for qualitative coding — descriptive, in vivo, process, emotion, values, and evaluation coding.
codebook.citEricsson1993: The foundational text on think-aloud protocols — establishes when verbal reports provide valid data about cognitive processes.

privacy.localBody: Bristlenose runs on your machine. Audio and video files never leave your computer. Transcript text is sent to your chosen LLM provider (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Azure OpenAI) for analysis — each provider’s data retention policy applies to those API calls.
privacy.localOllama: With Ollama (local models), nothing leaves your machine at all — not even transcript text. This is the most private option, but quality is lower than cloud models in 2026.
privacy.localAnon: Speaker codes (p1, p2) are the public-facing identity used in quotes, exports, and CSV files. Display names (first names) are visible in the app for your research team but are not included in exports. Anyone outside your team sees codes, not names.
privacy.redactionIntro: PII redaction is opt-in — enable it with --redact-pii. It’s off by default because false positives (redacting research-relevant text) damage data accuracy. The feature uses Microsoft Presidio, an open-source text scanner, with a configurable confidence threshold (BRISTLENOSE_PII_SCORE_THRESHOLD, default 0.7).
privacy.catchesBody: Full names in clear context (“My name is Sarah Thornton”), email addresses, phone numbers in standard formats, credit card numbers, Social Security numbers, IBANs, NHS numbers, and IP addresses.
privacy.missesBody: Non-Western names (the text analysis model was trained primarily on English text, so names from South Asian, East Asian, African, and Arabic naming traditions have lower detection rates). Nicknames and diminutives (“Bazza”, “Deano”). Names that are common words (“Grace”, “Will”, “Hope”). Misspelled names. Dictated contact details (“john dot smith at company dot co dot uk”). Phone numbers spoken in words. Social media handles. National Insurance numbers. Car registrations.
privacy.cannotBody: Sensitive personal disclosures — health conditions, ethnicity, political beliefs, religion, sexual orientation, and trade union membership. No automated tool reliably detects these in conversational speech. They require human review before sharing transcripts externally.
privacy.cannotIndirect: Indirect identification — individually harmless facts that together identify someone. For example, “38-year-old accessibility tester at a building society in Surrey” might narrow to one person. Job title combined with employer, a rare condition combined with a location, or a school name combined with a child’s age can all enable identification. This requires researcher judgement.
privacy.speakerIdNote: Note: speaker identification sends a small portion of raw transcript to the LLM before PII redaction runs, because speaker identification needs names and roles to work correctly. With Ollama, this stays on your machine.
privacy.actionReview: Always review the pii_summary.txt audit file after redaction — it lists everything that was detected and replaced, so you can check for false positives (over-redaction) or false negatives (things that were missed).
privacy.actionThreshold: For sensitive data, lower the confidence threshold (BRISTLENOSE_PII_SCORE_THRESHOLD=0.5) to catch more at the cost of more false positives.
privacy.actionSharing: Before sharing externally, review quotes for sensitive disclosures and indirect identifiers that automated tools cannot catch.
privacy.actionRetention: Your organisation’s data retention policy applies — delete output when the research purpose is complete.
privacy.actionNames: Display names don’t appear in exports, so you can safely use real first names during analysis without them reaching external stakeholders.
privacy.actionAudit: We publish a full PII redaction audit with a test transcript, redacted output, and raw audit log — so you can judge the limits for yourself.

signals.intro: The analysis page surfaces signals — statistically notable concentrations of sentiment or codebook tags within report sections. Two types of signal card.
signals.sentimentIntro: Each quote is tagged with one of seven sentiments designed for UX research:
signals.frustrationDesc: difficulty, annoyance, friction. Points to performance or interaction problems.
signals.confusionDesc: not understanding, uncertainty. Points to information architecture or labelling problems.
signals.doubtDesc: scepticism, worry, distrust. Points to credibility or trust problems. Separated from confusion because understanding something and trusting it are different — they require different design responses.
signals.surpriseDesc: expectation mismatch, neutral. Could be good or bad — only the researcher can tell from context.
signals.intensityScale: Each sentiment has an intensity scale of 1–3 (mild, moderate, strong). Quotes with no emotional content receive no sentiment tag.
signals.concentrationDesc: Concentration ratio: the observed rate of a sentiment in a section divided by its expected rate across the whole study — a ratio of 2× means that sentiment appears twice as often as you’d expect.
signals.agreementDesc: Agreement breadth (Simpson’s diversity index): how many participants share the signal, distinguishing group consensus from one person’s repeated reaction.
signals.intensityMetricDesc: Mean intensity: average strength on the 1–3 scale. These three are normalised and multiplied into a composite signal score.
signals.thresholds: Confidence thresholds: strong requires concentration above 2×, at least 5 participants, and at least 6 quotes; moderate requires above 1.5×, at least 3 participants, and at least 4 quotes; everything else is classified as emerging.
signals.frameworkBody: Framework signals use the same concentration math, but the columns are codebook groups (e.g. Norman’s Discoverability, Garrett’s Structure) instead of sentiments. Tag contributions are weighted: researcher-accepted tags count as 1.0, autocode proposals use the LLM’s confidence score, and denied proposals are excluded.
signals.patternTypesBody: For the top signals, an LLM call interprets the tagged quotes through each tag’s definition to produce an interpretive name, a pattern classification, and a one-sentence finding. Four pattern types: success (positive evidence — users’ expectations met), gap (negative — mismatch, friction, hidden features), tension (mixed positive and negative), and recovery (negative followed by positive — initial confusion, but users figure it out).
signals.citRussell2003: Valence and arousal are the two fundamental dimensions underlying all emotional experience.
signals.citBarrett2017: Emotions are constructed in the moment from core affect, conceptual knowledge, and context.
signals.citScherer2005: The Geneva Emotion Wheel — 20 emotion families based on appraisal theory.
signals.citBradley1994: Non-verbal pictorial scales for measuring pleasure, arousal, and dominance.
signals.citEkman1992: Six basic emotions universally recognised across cultures — influential but now contested by constructionist theories.
signals.citPlutchik1980: Eight primary emotions in opposing pairs with three intensity levels.
signals.citHassenzahl2003: Two UX quality dimensions: pragmatic (usability) and hedonic (stimulation, identity).
signals.citLaugwitz2008: The UEQ measures six dimensions: attractiveness, perspicuity, efficiency, dependability, stimulation, novelty.
signals.citBenedek2002: Product Reaction Cards — 118 words users select to describe their experience.
signals.citFogg2003: Users notice elements (prominence), then judge them (interpretation) — both steps must occur for credibility assessment.
signals.citFogg2001: Users judge credibility primarily on visual design and ease of use, not content accuracy.
signals.citFogg1999: Credibility = trustworthiness + expertise. Four types: presumed, surface, reputed, earned.
signals.citStanford2002: Ten research-based guidelines for building credible websites.
signals.citPicard1997: The founding text — computers that recognise and respond to emotion create more humane interaction.
signals.citDemszky2020: 58,000 comments labelled with 27 emotion categories — the largest fine-grained emotion dataset for NLP.

analysis.heading: Analysis
analysis.noData: No analysis data available. Run the pipeline or apply codebook tags to generate analysis.
analysis.sentimentDesc: Patterns ranked by signal strength — where sentiments concentrate more than expected given the study average.
analysis.tagDesc: Patterns ranked by signal strength — where codebook tags concentrate more than expected given the study average.
analysis.concTitle: Concentration ratio — how overrepresented vs study average
analysis.agreeTitle: Agreement — effective number of voices (Simpson's diversity)

codebook.heading: Codebook
codebook.description: Drag tags between groups to reorganise. Click a tag or title to rename it. Drop a tag on another to merge.
codebook.browseSubtitle: Import a framework codebook or create your own
codebook.restoreHelp: Restores previous tags and AutoCode results
codebook.importHelp: Adds to existing tags and tag-groups
codebook.tagsRemovedFromQuotes_one: Tags will be removed from {{count}} quote.
codebook.tagsRemovedFromQuotes_other: Tags will be removed from {{count}} quotes.
codebook.autoCodePreserved: AutoCode results are preserved — restore instantly from Browse Codebooks.
codebook.restoreAnytime: Restore any time from Browse Codebooks.
codebook.mergeBody: All quotes tagged {{source}} will be retagged {{target}}. This cannot be undone.
codebook.uncategorisedSubtitle: Tags not yet assigned to any group

review.title: AutoCode Review
review.dragInstruction: Drag the thresholds to control how many auto-tags are applied as tentative or accepted.
review.applyFailed: Apply failed — your per-row decisions are saved. Try again.

emptyState.prePipeline: Drop a folder of interviews to start analysis
emptyState.postZeroQuotes: Analysis finished but found no quotes. Check the source files or try a different LLM provider.
